questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of hesitancy, poor stream, and nocturia twice nightly. He has no pain or haematuria. Digital rectal examination (DRE) reveals a moderately enlarged, smooth, non-tender prostate. Serum PSA is 4.2 ng/mL. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of typical obstructive LUTS, symmetric smooth enlargement on DRE, and only mildly elevated PSA (commonly seen in BPH) makes BPH the most likely diagnosis. Prostate cancer would typically present with a hard, irregular nodule; chronic prostatitis involves pain; overactive bladder presents with urgency/frequency without hesitancy; urethral stricture often follows trauma or infection.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden inability to pass urine for 12 hours. He reports a long history of hesitancy and weak urinary stream. On examination, his bladder is distended and palpable. DRE reveals a symmetrically enlarged, firm but non-tender prostate. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute-on-chronic retention due to benign prostatic hyperplasia
questions.explanation: The gradual deterioration of obstructive symptoms over years followed by acute retention with a symmetrically enlarged, non-tender prostate points to acute-on-chronic retention from BPH. Acute prostatitis is exquisitely tender; bladder stones rarely cause sudden retention; urethral stricture usually has a history of instrumentation or infection; neurogenic bladder often has neurological signs.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a 5-year history of nocturia, frequency, and weak stream now presents with recurrent urinary tract infections and visible haematuria. Ultrasound shows a thickened bladder wall, post-void residual volume of 200 mL, and a 1 cm echogenic focus in the bladder. What is the single most likely underlying diagnosis?
options: Benign prostatic hyperplasia with bladder stone formation
questions.explanation: Chronic bladder outlet obstruction from BPH leads to detrusor hypertrophy, urinary stasis, and stone formation. The presence of a bladder stone with typical BPH symptoms and raised post-void residual makes BPH the underlying cause. Bladder cancer typically presents with painless haematuria without obstruction; prostate cancer with invasion would likely cause harder prostate; chronic prostatitis would include pain.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with bilateral leg swelling and fatigue. He denies any urinary symptoms. Blood tests show elevated creatinine (245 µmol/L) and urea. Urinalysis is bland. Renal ultrasound reveals bilateral hydronephrosis and a distended bladder. DRE demonstrates a moderately enlarged, firm prostate. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Chronic retention due to benign prostatic hyperplasia
questions.explanation: Chronic urinary retention from BPH can cause painless bilateral hydronephrosis and renal impairment in the absence of typical voiding symptoms (silent presentation). The smooth, symmetrical enlargement on DRE favors BPH over prostate cancer, which is typically irregular. Retroperitoneal fibrosis often presents with back pain and constitutional symptoms; bilateral stones would cause renal colic; neurogenic bladder usually has neurological deficits.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man who underwent a transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) 6 years ago now reports gradually worsening urinary stream and a sensation of incomplete bladder emptying. DRE reveals a small, smooth prostate. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: TURP does not remove all prostatic tissue; BPH can regrow over time causing recurrent obstructive symptoms. A small prostate on DRE does not exclude regrowth because only the central portion is typically enlarged. Bladder neck stenosis is less common and often presents earlier; urethral stricture typically follows instrumentation; prostate cancer would likely feel irregular; detrusor underactivity often presents with a sensation of incomplete emptying but weak stream, but the gradual onset after TURP favors regrowth.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with hesitancy, frequency, and nocturia. DRE reveals an enlarged, smooth prostate. Which tool is most appropriate to quantify the severity of his lower urinary tract symptoms?
questions.explanation: IPSS is the validated symptom score recommended by NICE to assess symptom severity and monitor response to treatment. PSA is used to rule out prostate cancer, not to quantify symptoms.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with LUTS has a PSA of 4.5 ng/mL and a normal DRE. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends multiparametric MRI as the first-line investigation for men with raised PSA and non-suspicious DRE, to detect clinically significant prostate cancer.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with LUTS undergoes uroflowmetry and post-void residual measurement. His flow rate is 8 mL/s (normal >15) and residual volume is 150 mL (normal <50). DRE is consistent with BPH. What is the most likely explanation for these findings?
options: Bladder outlet obstruction due to BPH
questions.explanation: Low flow rate with elevated residual is classic for bladder outlet obstruction. Detrusor underactivity would typically show low flow but normal residual or high residual with normal flow. The clinical context of BPH makes obstruction most likely.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with acute urinary retention. A catheter is inserted and drains 900 mL. He has a history of LUTS. DRE reveals an enlarged, smooth prostate. What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm that BPH is the cause of his retention?
questions.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound can assess prostate size, post-void residual, and hydronephrosis. It is the most appropriate initial imaging for suspected BPH causing acute retention.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with LUTS and a strong family history of prostate cancer has a normal DRE and PSA of 1.8 ng/mL. What is the most appropriate investigation to establish the diagnosis of BPH?
questions.explanation: Even with family history, a normal DRE and low PSA make prostate cancer unlikely. The IPSS is the first step to quantify symptom severity and confirm the diagnosis of BPH based on symptoms. PSA is not indicated for diagnosis of BPH.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man presents with mild lower urinary tract symptoms (IPSS 7). He has no bother and no complications. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.explanation: For mild symptoms (IPSS <8) without bother, watchful waiting is first-line. Alpha-blockers or 5-ARIs are indicated for moderate-severe symptoms, and TURP for complications or drug failure.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man has been taking tamsulosin 400 mcg daily for 6 months with only partial improvement. His IPSS is 18, and his prostate is estimated at 50 g on ultrasound. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends adding a 5-ARI (finasteride or dutasteride) if inadequate response to alpha-blocker and prostate >40 g. Increasing tamsulosin dose is not standard; TURP is considered after medical therapy fails; solifenacin is for storage symptoms.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man presents to the ED with acute urinary retention. He has a palpable bladder and is catheterised. His symptoms of BPH have been mild. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy to facilitate a trial without catheter (TWOC)?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends offering an alpha-blocker (e.g., tamsulosin) before TWOC. Surgery is considered if TWOC fails; 5-ARIs take months to work and are not useful acutely.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known BPH presents with visible haematuria. Cystoscopy reveals a large prostate with prominent vessels, no tumour. What is the most appropriate treatment to reduce recurrent bleeding?
questions.explanation: 5-ARIs (finasteride) reduce prostatic vascularity and haematuria from BPH. TURP is definitive but not first-line for bleeding alone; tranexamic acid does not address the underlying cause.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with BPH is currently taking tamsulosin 400 mcg daily. He is now scheduled for elective cataract surgery. Which of the following management changes is most appropriate?
options: Switch tamsulosin to alfuzosin 10 mg daily
Stop tamsulosin and restart after surgery
Switch tamsulosin to finasteride 5 mg daily
questions.explanation: Tamsulosin is associated with intraoperative floppy iris syndrome (IFIS); switching to an alternative alpha-blocker such as alfuzosin reduces risk. Finasteride does not affect the iris; stopping temporarily is not standard as the effect persists.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents to the GP with a 6-month history of urinary hesitancy, nocturia (×2), and a weak stream. He has no haematuria, dysuria, or weight loss. Urinalysis is negative. What is the SINGLE most appropriate next step in management?
options: Request a urinary flow rate and post-void residual volume
Refer for a urology outpatient appointment
questions.explanation: NICE recommends a digital rectal examination (DRE) and urinalysis as first-line assessment for all men with lower urinary tract symptoms. A PSA test is offered only after a DRE if prostate cancer is suspected, or if the patient requests it after counselling. Starting an alpha-blocker without a DRE is premature, as it could mask underlying malignancy.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) presents to the ED with acute urinary retention. He has a palpable bladder and is in significant discomfort. He last passed urine 10 hours ago. He is not on any current treatment for BPH. What is the SINGLE most appropriate immediate action?
options: Perform a bladder scan to confirm retention
Arrange a trial without catheter (TWOC) in 48 hours
questions.explanation: In acute urinary retention, immediate catheterisation is essential to relieve pain and prevent upper tract damage. A bladder scan is unnecessary when the bladder is clinically palpable. Starting an alpha-blocker is appropriate after catheterisation to facilitate a successful TWOC, but not the immediate step. Checking PSA is not urgent.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with BPH on tamsulosin presents to the ED with features of a urinary tract infection (dysuria, pyrexia 38.5°C) and new-onset acute urinary retention (suprapubic pain, palpable bladder). He has a white cell count of 18×10⁹/L and CRP of 120 mg/L. What is the SINGLE most appropriate immediate action?
options: Arrange an urgent CT scan of the urinary tract
questions.explanation: The priority is to relieve the acute retention, which is causing significant discomfort and may worsen sepsis. Catheterisation should be performed immediately; urine can be sent for culture and antibiotics started soon after. Stopping tamsulosin is not indicated. CT is not urgent in this setting.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with BPH and atrial fibrillation on warfarin presents to the ED with visible haematuria that started this morning. He has no pain or retention. His INR is 3.8. What is the SINGLE most appropriate immediate step?
options: Catheterise with a three-way catheter and start irrigation
Stop warfarin and give vitamin K
Check full blood count and urea/creatinine
Arrange a CT urogram for the next day
questions.explanation: A full blood count and renal function are essential to assess the severity of bleeding and guide further management. Stopping warfarin may be indicated, but this decision should be based on haemoglobin level and clinical status, not taken blindly. Catheterisation is not required unless there is clot retention. CT urogram is indicated but not immediate.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of mild LUTS: occasional hesitancy and frequency, but no nocturia. His IPSS score is 8. DRE reveals a moderately enlarged, smooth prostate. Urinalysis is normal. What is the SINGLE most appropriate next step?
options: Advise lifestyle modification and watchful waiting
Offer a transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP)
questions.explanation: NICE recommends lifestyle advice and watchful waiting for men with mild symptoms (IPSS ≤7) or moderate symptoms (8–19) that are not bothersome. An alpha-blocker is considered only if symptoms worsen or become bothersome after lifestyle measures. PSA is not routinely offered to asymptomatic men. TURP is reserved for severe or refractory cases.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents to his GP with a 6-month history of hesitancy, poor stream, and nocturia. He has no pain. What clinical finding on examination should prompt same-day urology referral?
questions.explanation: A palpable bladder after voiding indicates chronic urinary retention, which can cause renal impairment and requires urgent urological assessment. A hard irregular prostate suggests cancer but is not a same-day emergency. Haematuria and elevated PSA need referral but not immediately. Reduced anal tone points to neurological causes, not BPH.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with known BPH presents to the Emergency Department with acute urinary retention for 10 hours. He has a temperature of 38.5°C and is confused. Which of the following is the most likely life-threatening diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Fever with confusion in acute retention suggests urosepsis, a medical emergency requiring immediate antibiotics and catheterisation. Acute prostatitis is less severe without systemic sepsis. The other options do not cause fever with confusion.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man presents with LUTS typical of BPH. He also reports intermittent, painless haematuria. What is the most important diagnosis to exclude?
questions.explanation: Painless haematuria in an older man is a red flag for bladder cancer, even in the presence of BPH symptoms. Prostate cancer typically causes painless haematuria less frequently. Renal cell carcinoma often has flank pain or haematuria but is less common.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with long-standing BPH had an indwelling catheter inserted for acute retention. Twelve hours later his urine output is 250 mL/hour and he feels dizzy. What clinical finding indicates a potentially dangerous complication?
questions.explanation: High urine output after relief of obstruction (post-obstructive diuresis) can cause electrolyte disturbances and hypovolaemia. Hypotension is a later sign. Haematuria is common after catheterisation. Catheter bypassing suggests blockage.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with diabetes presents with LUTS including hesitancy and poor stream. He is on tamsulosin with limited improvement. What clinical finding on examination should raise suspicion for an alternative diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Reduced perianal sensation suggests cauda equina syndrome, which can cause neurogenic bladder mimicking BPH. This is a surgical emergency. The other findings are consistent with BPH or its complications.
